title: Factors and Effects of Trade Reorientation in Hungary
abstract: The collapse of the CMEA completed the Hungarian trade reorientation during the second half of the 1980s. Panel model estimations of trade reorientation reveal that cost efficiency, export subsidy and foreign demand played important and varying roles between 1981 and 1990. During the last two years cost efficiency ceased to exert an influence on the process, and subsidies were active instead.In 1991 the recession and the increased costs of trade reorientation led to a fall in the profitability of the corporate sector and exporting firms. Exporting firms with fast-growing exports had larger profits and they managed to combine the growth of exports with the expansion of domestic sales. Foreign capital and the new corporate forms have not yet ensured better performance.
summary_en: How did Hungary's trade reorientation away from CMEA toward Western markets actually unfold during 1981-1990? Halpern uses panel-model estimations to identify the relative roles of cost efficiency, export subsidies, and foreign demand in driving the reorientation. All three played important and varying roles, but with sharp dynamics: cost efficiency drove reorientation in the earlier 1980s, then ceased to influence the process; export subsidies became active in its place during the last two years before transition. By 1991, recession and rising trade-reorientation costs eroded corporate-sector profitability, but exporting firms with fast-growing exports retained higher profits. Foreign capital and new corporate forms had not yet delivered measurable performance gains by 1991.
data_used: Hungarian corporate-sector firm-level data 1981-1991 covering exporting firms. Panel-model estimation of trade-flow drivers; profit-rate analysis pre/post 1990.
policy_relevance: Historical baseline for understanding the early Hungarian transition. Useful retrospective for evaluating the relative roles of policy levers (subsidies) versus market forces (cost efficiency, demand) in driving structural change. Indirect relevance to current debates on government industrial-strategy interventions versus market-based reform. Caveat: 1980s context; modern Hungarian export structure is dominated by FDI-led integration.
